sentences.text: My name is Marvin Zborowski.
Z-B-O-R-O-W-S-K-I.
My middle name, [?
Henry. ?]
No.
I was born in Zarki, Poland.
Z-A-R-K-I.
I was born on June 4, 1928.
The town of Zarki was population 8,000, which 50% of the population were Jews.
The town was located 30 kilometers-- in other words, 20 miles-- from the town of Czestochowa, which was famous for the Black Madonna.
And to the south, we were close to town of Sosnowiec, which was a very industrial city.
My father's name was Moshe.
Zisel.
My father was in the leather business, and we had tanneries working for us, having the finished product back to our town.
Yes, I have an older brother and a younger sister.
The city itself was a quite vibrant city.
Consisted of all various affiliation and organizations.
I was too young to join, even too young to join the scouts.
I attended school.
I do remember my home quite vividly.
We lived quite comfortable.
We had a house, our own house, which is still in existence today.
Consisted of three rooms plus a kitchen.
Yes, we did have a household cleaning lady and a woman taking care of the children.
Our family was considered as religious.
We were devoted to the religion.
My father was attending services twice a day.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were going every Sabbath-- the children, with my father, we went to the synagogue.
We celebrated all the holidays.
And especially, I would outline the holidays of Pesach, which is Passover, where the family got together.
And I also had my grandparents in the same town, so I used to go, as a grandson, for Passover.
During the Tabernacle holidays of Sukkot, we were blessed that our home was quite-- considered, in those days, as a luxury.
We had a built-in tabernacle in the house, which is being called the sukkah.
And we even have guests in our home.
I would say, most of the-- most of the time, I would say Polish, but as well, Yiddish.
I attend cheder at the early age of four, five.
Then, of course, public school, which was quite obligatory to attend.

I do-- I did experience some kind of uneasiness, but I would say that it was not that the children did understand what means 'anti-Semitism.' If they came with a certain hatred or discomfort, it was only because probably, or most likely, they were-- they were taught at home, or being told at home.
Same thing goes on our behalf.
Coming from a Jewish home, I don't think so that I was free to associate with the Gentile children.
And the same, probably-- the Gentile children could not associate with the Jewish children.
Yes, I do remember vividly one incident, which was a very tragic one.
And this was in a summer month where school-- the public school-- was closed, and I attended cheder, which is the Hebrew school.
In the town arrived, in the marketplace, or to say, the center of the town-- a bus arrived.
In those days, a bus-- it was for me, as a child, a novice, to run to the bus and see it.
I mean, it's a bus, luxurious bus.
I didn't even had an opportunity to run closely to the bus, whereby 30 or 40 young men jumped out of the bus, just destroying Jewish property.
Just practically beating up Jewish people, which was a very unusual thing for me and will always remember in my memory.
Yes.
I was told later on that this was an organization.
They were wearing some kind of a uniform.
And that they belonged to a certain extreme organization which were the anti-Jewish.
I would not say 'anti-Semitism.' They were anti-Jewish.
I did not understand the full meaning of the word 'anti-Semitism.'
We did-- always, my father-- my late father was always speaking about the danger, obviously since the time of Hitler coming to power.
And being that our town was so close to the German border, we were always worried about it.
But this was not the question that we were aware.
We were aware.
But still and all, not every house had a radio.
Not every house had a telephone.
And, in turn, we were limited even with the newspapers, so to say.
Even though that the government of Poland, as I read, later years, between 1920 and 1939, it was most of the time was not a free country.
It was a dictatorship under the Polish government, where no actual publications or papers could be printed that their own way of thinking.
Besides, I would say people had-- even if they were aware of anti-Semitism, there was no way where to run.

From later time that I was told, a later date, that to Palestine you could only go if you were either belonging to an organization that's one of the scouts organization, or from the extreme left of the halutzim, which means 'the pioneers.' Or the ones, let's say, like my father, having a family-- he could have also emigrated by showing that he has means to live in Israel.
In other words, a certain amount of money, which will give him the preference of going to Israel and settling, cultivate the land of Israel.
At those days, of course, not Israel-- it was Palestine.
I do remember quite well.
The town was a social-oriented and cultural-oriented town.
Starting with all the organizations, from extreme left to the extreme right, were in the town.
Children had places where to go.
We especially, being at a young age, we were very comfortable at home.
We were considered one of the, so to say, wealthy people.
Having a home, living in-- quite comfortable, having to eat quite comfortable.

And even, we had-- every day we had, from dawn, we had a higher school of learning in Jewish studies, whereby a yeshiva boy, which means a boy which attended the higher studies, Jewish studies, came to our home and everyday, to have a lunch, which at those day was considered as a dinner.
Yes, my father was very charitable.
And besides being charitable, he always believed of helping people [SPEAKING YIDDISH] which, in other words, it has the meaning of helping people to establish themselves.
Not just giving outright as well, but giving people to be able to sustain and live a better life.
That's correct.
Yes, I do remember.
It was September 1, on a Friday.
We did not even realize.
People were just preparing for the Sabbath.
School year just started.
It was very festive.
It was before the High Holidays.
Nobody was-- even though with the news that were coming that Germany is really arming themself, we still couldn't believe that it is so close.
Still and all, September 1, the war broke out.
On September 2, which was on a Sabbath, the town was practically completely destroyed by bombs.
I don't know exactly how many planes, but the city was destroyed by bombs-- destructive and firebombs.
The town was practically in flames.
There were a lot of the casualties at that day.
The most interesting part is why they had to bomb this town, which was no industry.
But five kilometers, meaning two and a half miles, was the heaviest industry of the country of Poland-- was not touched.
Meaning, in other words, they knew that this belongs to them, just to create the havoc of people [? of ?] the scariness of war.
They bombed the surrounding areas, not the industrial areas.
On Sunday, the second day, so to say, of the war-- the first, second Sunday-- they just simply went through, like-- like, just driving through the town.
The Germans came in, and they occupied the town, of course, creating various scary shootings, plundering everything possible.
From the beginning.

This seemingly-- as I was, at that time, being a boy of 11, not realizing what means 'SS,' and not realizing what means 'Wehrmacht,' or what means 'Germans,' to me, a motorcycle or the cars were the interesting part to go and see.
Seemingly, I could not go and see because, just at the sight, they were just killing.
So everybody was in hiding.
Not the question of Jewish population or-- the population of the town, period.
Then, on Monday-- this is in the history, written many books that I found after the war-- they were considering this the Bloody Monday.
The reason the Bloody Monday was that they came into town, seemingly the black uniforms-- which later on, we found out that this is the SS 'Sturmtruppen,' so to say.
They were killing-- just merciless, merciless, wherever, just-- just killing.
Jews and Gentile, but most of them Jews that I could say.
Because the Jews lived in an area in that small town close, so to say, within the vicinity.
So the center of that town was Jewish, so this whole center was destroyed.
But a lot of killings, a lot of people.
And this was-- I was told later on, also, that this was not in our town.
It's the whole area of the Czestochowa area, all towns went through the same thing.
I would say that there was no official government.

One thing I have to say-- even though that I am Jewish, but at the same time, I'm born in Poland-- there was no Polish government which collaborated with the Germans in comparison of later days and years.
That the Slovakian army or government had collaborated, Hungary had a government that collaborated, Norway had a government that collaborated, France--
At a later date, I would say that they let the people run the, so to say, the city council, which consisted mostly of Gentiles.
And the Jews again now established their own committee, which was also approved by the Germans.
Life changed from day one.
Everyday, there were new-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [?
[SPEAKING POLISH] ?]
--new orders--
--ordinances how to make life more difficult, and--
The life of difficulty was, first of all, depriving the people to exercise their profession.
Most of the people were businesspeople.
So therefore, they could not do any commerce.
Most of the businesses were confiscated.
If it was larger enterprises, they put in a [? treuhander, ?] which I would say, it's a governing person which was watching over your business.
Our business was confiscated.
They came with trucks, load it, and everything.
I do even remember that there was not the question that they were plundering, and robbing, and taking away processions.
The Germans, they took everything.
In 1938, we finished our public school.
It was a new school that new benches, that I don't think so that even today is in existence.
Which I do remember distinctly, each individual student had his own bench, with high polish.
This school-- all the furnitures from that school was, in the first week of the war, taking out and sent to Germany.
So don't ever think that they did not know where it coming from.
They were plundering, confiscating.
Whatever it was movable, they took it.
Not at all.
So this-- this is the next step, how they try to humiliate the people.
In other words, by taking away the sustenance and to be able to support themselves, there were no humane ways.
Then, of course, people had to risk their lives and go out of the town, bringing in just staple food like potatoes, flour, just simply to be able to sustain and live.
Then, of course, came the ordinance of giving away personal possessions.
Like, for instance, jewelry.
So, jewelry-- people were not so eager or fast to give, so they put this in hiding, and it was easy to hide jewelry.
Nobody's going to be that stupid to give away diamonds if they had diamonds, or gold if they had gold.
Then they starting to come out with an ordinance-- being that they were preparing for the war with Russia, they took away all the furs that the people had.
Not the question of fur coats or fur lining-- anything that they had.
Even if a coat had a fur collar, you had to strip your collar and deliver the collar to the Germans.
It was [?
Sammelstelle-- ?] that means to say, it was a collecting place.

And it was written down that 'This and this family gave so many of so many.' In other words, they knew-- or they were probably denouncing that, let's say, if a family was a well-to-do family, that that family probably has more than one piece or two pieces of fur, so to say, to be delivered.

I don't know exactly who did, but they must have some kind of a system to find out who is well, and who is poor-- some-- I could not imagine-- even if somebody will be denouncing it, seemingly, that they were threatening that they do know.
Could possibly also be that-- that they did not know, and they're just pretending that they do know.
I do remember exactly an incident when it comes to this question of furs.
As I said, the people-- most of the people in the town were religious people.
And, being religious, you had to go twice to-- to the prayer-- for prayers to the synagogue.
And at one time-- which, of course, all the synagogue was destroyed in 1939, with the exception of the big synagogue.
But then, they established small synagogues in private houses, because you're not supposed to just gather together.
Being that our customs is that by having 10 people you could establish a formal prayer.
So a man was walking to a prayer.
And he was probably-- was heavy [INAUDIBLE] this man, a sick man, and he was wearing fur pants.
And the Germans spotted that he is wearing pants which are fur-lined.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was at the time when the ordinance was that all the furs has to be delivered to the Germans.
They did not ask him to take off the-- the pants.
They did not ask him-- they just told him, 'You're wearing fur pants, which they're supposed to have been delivered.' They killed him on the spot.
Then they did in a very humiliating way-- they gave ordinance, if you give so much and so much contribution of money, we will let you be free.
In other words, free in a sense we will not bother you that you should give so many so many men for work, or so many, many more restrictions, or you get more rations.
Don't forget, we-- all-- all the food, whatever meager part, was given to us by rations.
In other words, we had little pieces of paper for rations.
The-- the contributions that-- that we had to give or, so to say, to be bailed out.
Let's say this town population-- let's say 4,000 Jews-- had to deliver by the date so-and-so-- has to deliver, let's say, 1 million [? zloty, ?] or 100,000, or whatever amount.
By that date, that money had to be delivered.
People were given rations, to-- to-- that they could get, let's say, a pound or two pounds of bread.
I do not recall the exactly weigh.
I definitely did not go to pick up my-- my portion or whatever.
I would say they were giving--
I would say they were registered.
I did not have any ID card whatsoever throughout my time, being, during the war.
Yes, there were.
People were helping.
My home, that I was brought up, that we always helped.
Because we were fortunate to have certain reserves of money that we could help people.
But I do remember at the time that 300 Jews were put into our town from-- were expelled from the north into our town.
And it was on a Friday, or Thursday or Friday, and they were distributed throughout the town to people who had, let's say, larger homes.
So they put in a family or whatever.
And whatever they could not allocate any rooms, they were actually in the gym and the school.
And they were quartered there, given some sleeping facilities that they should live there.
First of all, also, I think that I overlooked one step, that all the Jews had to wear the bands with the David star.
Some other areas-- let's say, in our town, was the border, the German borders.
On the other side of the border, they were wearing yellow David stars with written 'Jude,' which means 'Jews.'
I had to wear a band with the-- with the Star of David.
No.
Radios had-- they were confiscated in the first days, that we should be practically cut off from-- from any news.
The community itself was stricken with various epidemic due to the situation and the hygienic problems.
Therefore, we could not be-- have any possibility to-- to-- People were dying.
They were decimated.
People were really without any means of living, and so on.
They were really very, very poor conditions.
